coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots anchored onto the metal-organic framework NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) as a novel visible light-driven photocatalyst: Photocatalytic performance and mechanism investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Liangyan
Yu, Zongxue
Li, Xiuhui
Li, Xuyang
Zeng, Haojie
Feng, Xiaofang
coredata.dc:description: A one-pot strategy for the preparation of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composite was successfully developed via a simple solvothermal synthesis method. NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, XPS, UV–vis DRS, PL, transient photocurrent response and Mott-Schottky plots. The synthesized NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites exhibit stronger photocatalytic activity than the original NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) in both photoreduction of Cr(VI) and photodegradation of MB. This is mainly because on the one hand, there is an interaction at the interface between CDs and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), and on the other hand, CDs in the composite as local electron acceptors can effectively enhance charge transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination. The experimental results show that the pH value greatly affects the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) over photocatalyst, and the addition of hole trapping agent is beneficial to photocatalytic reduction. Moreover, we have proposed the possible photocatalytic mechanism on composites through trapping experiments of active species. Importantly, cycling experiments have also revealed that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs photocatalysts have good chemical stability and reusability, and have broad industrial application prospects.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots anchored onto the metal-organic framework NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) as a novel visible light-driven photocatalyst: Photocatalytic performance and mechanism investigation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-pot strategy for the preparation of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composite was successfully developed via a simple solvothermal synthesis method. NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, XPS, UV–vis DRS, PL, transient photocurrent response and Mott-Schottky plots. The synthesized NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites exhibit stronger photocatalytic activity than the original NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) in both photoreduction of Cr(VI) and photodegradation of MB. This is mainly because on the one hand, there is an interaction at the interface between CDs and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), and on the other hand, CDs in the composite as local electron acceptors can effectively enhance charge transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination. The experimental results show that the pH value greatly affects the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) over photocatalyst, and the addition of hole trapping agent is beneficial to photocatalytic reduction. Moreover, we have proposed the possible photocatalytic mechanism on composites through trapping experiments of active species. Importantly, cycling experiments have also revealed that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs photocatalysts have good chemical stability and reusability, and have broad industrial application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: A huge amount of inorganic heavy metal ions and toxic organic compounds produced in many industrial processes, for instance, steel mill, metallurgical industry, leather, electroplating industry, printing, cosmetics, textiles, pharmaceuticals, et al., with high toxicity and potential environmental pollution, carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic characteristics, causing diverse adverse health effects and serious environmental problems, which have attracted worldwide attention [1–6]. Taking organic dyes, for example, they are being used in a plethora of products e.g. paper, textile, plastic, and cosmetics industries primarily because of their bright clear color and ease of use, in which most of the wastewater produced by the textile industry contains high concentrations of dyes, ranging from 10 to 200 mg L−1 [7–10]. The release of these toxic, chemically stable, non-biodegradable and unpleasantly colored organic materials into the aquatic environment is alarming. Another water contaminant, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), is one of the typical heavy metal ions, it is with high levels of toxicity and non-biodegradable, and may also cause DNA damage and cancers [11–13]. Besides, Cr(VI) is difficult to be aggregated and removed because it has high solubility in water. In contrast to Cr(VI), Cr(III) is essentially immobile and can be adsorbed or precipitated on varieties of substrates in alkaline and neutral solutions [14]. More importantly, Cr(III) is eco-friendly and has a positive relationship to human life and plants [15]. Therefore, the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) via photocatalytic technology is considered to be a relatively effective method in wastewater treatment.

Photocatalysis is considered to be an economic, green and effective method for the removal of a variety of water pollutants due to its benign nature and low cost involved [16]. In more recent years, many semiconductor photocatalysts such as TiO2, CdS, ZnO and WO3 have been found to be used for degradation of contaminants in photocatalytic systems [17,18]. Although they have great potential in Cr(VI) reduction and degradation of organic compounds, there are still some undesirable features such as UV-restricted activation, photocorrosion, and severe recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes [17,19]. Consequently, for purpose of increase the activity of the photocatalyst and more effectively utilize sunlight, it is necessary to develop a novel visible light-driven photocatalyst with high activity, suppressed charge recombination and environmental benignity for the treatment of wastewater.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as a new class of emerging porous crystalline materials obtained by assembly of metal ions (or metal clusters) and organic building blocks, have exhibited a wide range of applications in the fields of gas storage/separation, drug delivery, sensing, catalysis as well as proton conductivity due to their high surface area, well-defined pores structures, opened channels and rich active sites [20–23]. In recent years, some studies have shown that certain MOFs behave as semiconductors when exposed to light, which means that they are potentially useful as photocatalyst [24]. Compared with the traditional photocatalysts, the advantages of MOFs as photocatalysts is that the porous structure and high surface area promote rapid diffusion of substrates and products through the opened channels. In addition, the bandgap of the MOFs is significantly associated with the HOMO-LUMO gap, and can be flexibly adjusted by reasonable modification of the inorganic component or the organic ligand in the synthesis process, thereby achieving efficient light harvesting [25]. Mahata et al. reported that different Zn, Ni, and Co-based MOFs have the ability to degrade organic dyes under UV light [26]. Antibiotics can be effectively degraded by In2S3@MIL-125(Ti) synthesized by Wang et al [27]. Although these reports demonstrate the potential of MOFs photocatalysts for the degradation of contaminants in water, unfortunately, most MOFs photocatalysts also suffer from shortcomings in poor visible light capturing and rapid charge recombination of photogenerated carriers, which dramatically limit the photocatalytic performance of MOFs.

In the previous years, numerous effective strategies have been adopted to improve the separation of photo-generated charge carriers of MOFs, such as noble metal deposition, coupling with inorganic or organic semiconductors, as well as modification by conductive carbon nanomaterials including graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) [28–30]. Carbon nanodots (CDs), an emerging light-absorbing nanomaterial with unique physicochemical properties, including tunable photoluminescence, fast electron transfer capability, and efficient light harvesting, has been recognized as a promising alternative cocatalyst for applications in photocatalysis [31–33]. CDs as cocatalysts can play various roles in photocatalytic systems, such as photosensitizer, spectral converter, electron acceptor, and the like [34]. Although the use of CDs in combination with some inorganic photocatalysts to improve catalytic activity has been widely applied, their application as localized electron acceptor grown in situ on the surface of MOFs has rarely been reported so far.

Herein, CDs are generated in situ by a simple solvothermal method on the surface of a typical MOF photocatalyst (NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)). The morphology, structure, chemical composition, photoactivity and optical properties of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were characterized systematically. Then, the photocatalytic performance was evaluated by aqueous Cr(VI) reduction and methylene blue (MB) in degradation at ambient temperature under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, the effects of the addition of active substance capture agents, the pH value of the reaction solution and various operational factors on the reduction of Cr(VI) were also researched. Finally, based on the experimental results, the mechanism of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and MB degradation and the possible role of CDs are discussed.

2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, >98%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. FeCl3·6H2O (>99%), K2S2O8 (>99.5%), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 98.5%), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, >99%), tert-butanol (t-BuOH, >99%), glucose anhydrous, potassium dichromate (≥99.8%), methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S·3H2O, ≥99.8%), 1, 5-diphenylcarbohydrazide (DPC), acetone (≥99.5%), phosphoric acid (≥85%), N, N′-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5%) and methanol (CH3OH, ≥99.5%) from Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd., Cheng du, China. All the reagents were of commercially available analytical grade unless otherwise noted, and used without further purification.

NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) was prepared via solvothermal process previously reported in the literature [35,36]. Firstly, 2.497 mmol FeCl3·6H2O was added into 7.5 mL DMF solution and dissolved; Secondly, 1.242 mmol of NH2-BDC was dissolved in another 7.5 mL of DMF solution; Thirdly, the two solutions were mixed and sonicated for 10 min and then heat treated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 110 °C for 24 h; Finally, after naturally cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with DMF and CH3OH, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h for further use.

Preparation of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites was identical to the synthesis of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) except that an appropriate amount of anhydrous glucose (30 mg, 50 mg, and 70 mg) was added in the first step. Among them, the corresponding NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were designated as NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-30, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50, and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were monitored on a FTIR spectrometer (FTIR; WQF-520) using the KBr pellet technique. The crystal structure was characterized using a X'Pert Pro diffractometer (PANalytical) under Cu Kα radiation at a scan rate of 2°/min ranging from 5° to 70°. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was recorded on Hitachi S-4800 SEM. The microstructure and morphology of the photocatalyst were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on JEOL 2000F TEM. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was monitored by PerkinElmer Lambda 850 UV–vis-NIR spectrometer with BaSO4 as the internal standard. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) was applied to analyze the surface elemental compositions and electronic states. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were detected at room temperature by a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 DC-0506) with excitation of 325 nm wavelength. Mott-Schottky plots and transient photocurrent response of samples [deposited in ITO glass] were tested in a three-electrode system by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). Changes in MB and Cr(VI) concentrations were monitored by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and MB degradation experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites was investigated via photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) and degradation of MB solution at room temperature. The initial concentration of Cr(VI) (based on Cr in dilute K2Cr2O7 solution) and MB were maintained at 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L, respectively. And the pH of aqueous Cr(VI) was adjusted to a suitable value with diluted H2SO4 solution. 20 mg of the photocatalyst was added to 100 mL of Cr(VI) and MB solution, respectively, and placed in a dark environment magnetic stirring for 40 min or 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A 500 W Xe lamp coupled with a cutoff filter was acted as a visible light source, and the reaction solution containing the photocatalyst was placed at 20 cm away from the light source. 5 mL of the solution was taken out from the reaction system every 15 min and the photocatalyst was removed by centrifugation. Since Cr(VI) forms a purple colored complex with diphenylcarbazide (DPC) in an acidic medium and has a maximum absorption at 540 nm, the concentration of Cr(VI) was estimated by measuring the absorbance at 540 nm using UV–Vis spectroscopy [37]. Similarly, changes in MB concentration were also recorded by measuring the absorbance of the solution at 664 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The photocatalytic efficiency was determined by dividing Ct/C0. The reaction kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction and MB degradation can be attributed to simple first-order kinetic reactions and was calculated using the formula below.
l n (
C 0
/
C t
) = k · t
where C0 is the initial concentration of Cr(VI) and MB; Ct is remained concentration of Cr(VI) and MB in solution at each given time; k is the apparent first-order kinetics rate constant; t represent reaction time.

In order to analyze the corresponding photocatalytic reaction mechanism, various scavengers include K2S2O8 (1 μM), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 1 μM), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 1 μM) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH, 1 μM) was introduced into the reaction system to trap electrons (e−), holes (h+), superoxide anion radicals (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (
OH), respectively. Thereby, the main active substances which have an influence on NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were determined.

XRD patterns of as-prepared NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites and the pristine NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) are presented in Fig. 1 . The characteristic diffraction peaks of as-prepared NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) appeared at approximately 9.2° (0 0 2), 10.3° (1 0 1), 13.1° (1 0 2), 16.7° (1 0 3), 18.5° (2 0 0), 19.1° (2 0 1), 20.7° (2 0 2), 26.3° (2 0 4), and 29.5° (3 0 2) are all consistent with the simulated XRD pattern of the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) structure established according to the crystallographic information file (CIF) (Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre (CCDC) 647646) [35]. It indicates that the pure product with NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) topology was synthesized successfully. In comparison, the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites reveal similar locations of peaks to NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), whereas its peak intensity gradually becomes weak. This illustrates that the framework of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) can still be maintained after introducing the precursor glucose of the CDs solvothermal. What needs special attention is that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 only has one broad and weak peak at about 9°, which means that excess glucose affects the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) content and crystallization. There are no obvious diffraction peaks belonging to CDs, which may suggest that they are almost amorphous.

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 ) was carried out to further investigate the molecular structures and functional groups of the parent NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites. In NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), the absorption peaks around 3464 and 3324 cm−1 belongs to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of NH2 groups [38,39]. Furthermore, two peaks at 1576 and 1384 cm−1 are attributed to typical carboxyl groups vibrations, respectively [40,41]. The vibration band at 1255 cm−1 is caused by the CN stretching mode in the benzene ring. The CH bending vibration in the organic linkers can be observed at 769 cm−1 [42]. Meanwhile, the vibrational band of OFeO formed by Fe(III) and organic ligands clearly appeared at 519 and 443 cm−1 [43]. For the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites, a stronger absorption peak at 670 cm−1 is most likely correspond to the in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations of the COO groups [39]. Furthermore, a new peak at 2920 cm−1 appears on the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70, which is ascribed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of CH2 and thus reveals the presence of CDs. In general, the FT-IR spectrum of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites is approximately the same as NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), which means that the introduction of CDs does not substantially change the basic attributes of MOFs.

Morphology and microstructure of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites were observed by FE-SEM combined with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) consists of hexagonal microspindle crystals with a length of about 1 μm and a width of about 400 nm, similar to that of previously reported literature [14]. For NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites, when a small amount of glucose precursor is added (Fig. 3b), the surface of the parent NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) is covered with a layer of material and anchored with a small amount of CDs. With the increase of the precursor (Fig. 3c), CDs with different sizes adhered tightly to the surface of Fe-MOF. However, it is observed that the distribution of CDs is very uneven, which is likely to have an impact on the photocatalytic properties of the materials. When glucose is excessive (Fig. 3d), a large number of fine CDs are agglomerated together, and almost no evident Fe-MOF was discovered, which inosculates well with the results of FT-IR and XRD. Falco et al. found that 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) was abundantly formed during the early stages of the hydrothermal treatment of glucose, which subsequently undergoes a series of polymerization-condensation reactions to form polyfuranic type compounds [44]. As shown by SEM, the polyfuranic type compounds were adhered to the surface of the generated MOF, and as its content increases, it forms nano/micron sized fragments which eventually form spherical envelopes. EDS point scan spectra (Fig. 3e, f) further confirmed that the irregular small-sized particles are CDs and the bulk structure is Fe-MOF, because CDs not only do not contain N element, but also have higher C content than the main material.

The morphology of the obtained samples before and after CDs generation was further investigated by TEM and HRTEM. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a, the pristine MOF side profile appears as hexagon. Furthermore, the top profile indicates that the Fe-MOF is in the shape of octahedron, which is in accord with the SEM characterization. In the high-resolution images (Fig. 4b, c), there are NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) crystals that are not fully grown, which also explains the phenomenon that the Fe-MOF size is not equal. For NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 (Fig. 4d, e, f), after CDs were generated in situ, the complete crystal structure of MOFs was reserved and many CDs of 5–50 nm in size were attached to the surface. These results indicate that CDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of Fe-MOF, which facilitates the rapid transfer of photoexcited charge during photocatalysis and further enhances the photocatalytic performance of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe).

The surface electronic states and chemical composition of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 relative to NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) were further investigated by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 5 . The full survey spectrum (Fig. 5a) displays that both materials contain C, O, N and Fe. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 5b) can be split into three main species: (1) the alkyl carbon components of the benzene ring at 284.6 eV, (2) the C-N bond on the organic linker at 285.1 eV, and (3) the carboxylate carbon in COO groups at 288.5 eV [45–47]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 398.9 eV and 400.3 eV, corresponding to CN bonds and amino NH bonds on the NH2-BDC, respectively [48]. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 5d) shows two main peaks and one satellite peak, the peak at 711.7 eV is attributed to Fe 2p3/2, the peak at 725.7 eV is ascribed to Fe 2p1/2, and the peak at 714.4 eV is assigned to the satellite signal [49]. In addition, 2p1/2-2p3/2 = 14 eV, peak separation is very similar to those reported for Fe2O3 [50]. Results of all of these indicate that Fe3+ is the main iron species present in the composite. Since preparation of CDs with anhydrous glucose as a precursor does not introduce new elements, the XPS spectrum of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 is basically consistent with NH2-MIL-88B(Fe). Unexpectedly, a new peak at 405.9 eV emerged in the N 1s spectrum of the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 sample. This is most likely N-O bonds formed by the interaction of oxygen-containing functional groups in glucose with the NH2 groups on NH2-BDC during the hydrothermal reaction [51,52]. It is shown that interaction exists on the interface between CDs and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe). According to XPS results, it is speculated that the introduction of CDs is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic performance of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe).

The specific surface area of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites was investigated by nitrogen physisorption method. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , based on the IUPAC classification, the isotherms of the parent NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and all NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites are close to Type IV, indicating that these materials have obvious mesoporous structure features [53,54]. Interestingly, the isotherm of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 has a hysteresis loop with a shape close to Type H3 at high relative pressure between 0.4 and 1.0, which means the presence of slit-like mesopores or macroporous materials [55]. Table 1 shows that the prepared NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) had a BET surface area of 59.4641 m2/g and an average pore diameter of 30.3893 nm. A possible explanation for this phenomenon is that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) with low BET surface area and closed pore upon thermal activation is not available to N2-physisorption measurements [56]. Meanwhile, the BET surface area was reduced from 59.4641 m2/g of pure MOF to 40.3196 m2/g of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-30 and 29.7615 m2/g of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50. The decrease in surface area may be related to the increase in the average particle size [57]. The BET surface area of ​​NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 is 114.9316 m2/g, which is about twice that of pure MOF. The maximum BET surface area and the smallest pore size distribution of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 are likely due to the formation of micropores on the surface of CDs and MOF after loading of CDs [58].

In order to evaluate the optical response characteristics and optical bandgap of as-synthesized samples, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) with different CDs-loading was collected, as displayed in Fig. 7 a. The spectrum of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) exhibits a broad absorption band of 200–800 nm, indicating that it has strong ultraviolet and visible light harvesting ability. The strong absorption in the ultraviolet region is attributed to the π-π* transition of the organic linker, the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) corresponds to absorption in the visible region [59,60]. The prepared NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites retained a wide range of visible light absorption compared with the original MOF. Moreover, the spectra of these composites can be deconvolved into two bands, one with a wavelength of 200–360 nm attributed to CDs and the other with a wavelength of 360–800 nm ascribed to NH2-MIL-88B(Fe). In summary, visible light capture capability of composite materials is mainly due to MOF.

Meanwhile, the bandgap energy of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 was estimated by the energy dependence relation αhν=(hν-Eg)1/2, where Eg and α represent the bandgap energy and absorption coefficient of semiconductor, respectively [61]. The results are given in Fig. 7b, the Eg of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 are estimated to be 2.03 eV and 1.98 eV, respectively. Both samples have such similar Eg values, which mean that the loading of CDs on the surface of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) does not affect the band structure of the original MOF.

As known, prerequisites for photocatalytic reactions are the effective generation and instant separation of photogenerated electron-hole carriers, since photoluminescence (PL) originates from the recombination of photoinduced charges, the charge separation efficiency of the samples was analyzed using photoluminescence emission spectroscopy (Fig. 8 ) [62]. The PL intensity of composites is greatly quenched compared with the parent MOF, indicating that CDs effectively increase the transport pathway of electrons and thereby reduce the recombination of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. In addition, we found that when the CDs are overloaded, the PL signal is instead enhanced. This may be due to excessive glucose precursors that affect the production of MOF, as evidenced by previous XRD and SEM. Interestingly, the luminescence emission of the composites showed varying degrees of blueshift compared with pure MOF, which is most likely due to the size of the CDs [63]. It is worth noting that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 obtained the lowest PL signal, which we speculated to have the best photocatalytic activity.

Since the lifetime and separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers can be measured by the transient photocurrent response under light illumination, so we obtained the transient photocurrent response curves of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 in a three-electrode system (Fig. 9 a). Normally, higher photocurrent intensity means more efficient photoinduced charge carrier separation [64]. Obviously, the photocurrent density of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 is significantly higher than that of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), which again reveals that the modification of CDs can change the surface charges of Fe-MOF and ultimately extend the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers. This result is consistent with the XPS and PL spectra, suggesting that the loading of CDs has a positive effect on improving the photocatalytic efficiency of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe).

Fig. 9b displays Mott-Schottky plots of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 to illustrate their electronic band structure. Both materials present a positive slope, which implies they are all n-type semiconductors [65]. The flat band potential of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 (vs. SCE, pH = 6.8) was measured to be approximately −0.39 eV and −0.40 eV, equaling to −0.15 eV and −0.16 eV vs NHE (pH = 6.8). This result confirmed that the introduction of CDs did not substantially affect the band structure of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe), and the flat band potential of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) was similar to that of the literature [39]. Since the conduction band (ECB) of most n-type semiconductors is more negative than the flat band potential by about 0.1 eV, the conduction bands of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 are −0.25 eV and −0.26 eV, respectively. Since the Eg value was obtained by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy, based on the empirical formula Eg = EVB-ECB, the valence bands (EVB) of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 were calculated to be 1.73 eV and 1.77 eV, respectively.

We evaluated the ability of Fe-MOF with different CDs loading to reduce Cr(VI) under visible light conditions, as seen in Fig. 10 . As we have previously speculated, the introduction of CDs can greatly increase the activity of NH2-MIL-88B (Fe), and the reaction rates followed an ascending order of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) < NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-30 < NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 < NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50. After 40 min of treatment in the dark, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 achieved the optimum adsorption rate of approximately 13%. However, after 60 min of visible light irradiation, all NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites can completely reduce Cr(VI), while the original MOF can only reduce 66%. Among them, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 reduced 95% of Cr(VI) when exposed to light for 45 min, and the corresponding reaction rate (k = 0.05491 min−1) is almost 3.3 times as high as pure MOF (k = 0.01648 min−1). In general, the photocatalytic activity of the composites enhances with the increase of CDs loading, when an optimal value is reached, excessive CDs will be detrimental to its activity. This phenomenon is due in part to the fact that an excess of the cocatalyst can serve as possible recombination centers for the e−/h+ pairs in the photocatalytic system, and on the other hand, excessive CDs on the surface of the photocatalyst may shield the incident light [18].

Since Cr(OH)3 colloid is the main form of Cr(III) in alkaline aqueous solution, it may cover the surface of photocatalyst and affect its photocatalytic activity. Therefore, we mainly investigate the reduction activity of photocatalysts on Cr(VI) in acidic solution. As depicted in Fig. 11 a and b, photocatalytic reduction efficiency of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 on Cr(VI) is strongly influenced by pH, and it will increase significantly as the pH value is lowered. This phenomenon is due to that the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) is an acid-catalyzed behavior [66]. Specifically, in an acidic environment, the surface of photocatalyst carried positive charge due to its adsorption of a large amount of H+ ions. At the same time, Cr2O7 2− and HCr2O7 − are the main forms of Cr(VI) in an acidic solution [67]. Therefore, Cr(VI) can be effectively adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst due to electrostatic interaction, and finally improved photocatalytic activity, which is why the higher the acidity, the higher the adsorption efficiency.

In order to more intuitively evaluate the photocatalytic effect, Fig. 11c shows the time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectrum of the DPC-Cr(VI) complex solution after reduction by NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 at pH = 2. It shows that as the irradiation time increases, the absorption peak of DPC-Cr(VI) complex at 540 nm gradually decreases and reaches a minimum at 45 min, which indicates that Cr(VI) in the reaction solution has been all reduced to Cr(III). In addition, it can be observed that the solution has basically reached the adsorption-desorption equilibrium after adsorption for 40 min, indicating that the reduction of Cr(VI) is mainly due to photocatalysis rather than adsorption.

In order to more comprehensively evaluate the activity of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites, the ability of as-prepared photocatalysts to degrade organic pollutant was investigated by using MB as a representative dye. Fig. 11d shows that after 30 min of dark treatment, the pure MOF can only adsorb 11% of MB, but the optimum adsorption capacity of composites can reach 45%, which perhaps due to the modification of CDs to increase the specific surface area of ​​the material. The MB degradation efficiency of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) was 58% after visible light irradiation for 90 min, and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-30, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 and NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-70 were 87%, 92% and 82%, respectively. Fig. 11e shows that the reaction rate of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 (k = 0.0220 min−1) is almost 2.6 times higher than that of pure MOF (k = 0.0085 min−1). The degradation of MB by composites is similar to the reduction of Cr(VI), which indicates that loading a certain amount of CDs contributes to the improvement of photocatalytic activity, and the excessive loading is not conducive to photocatalysis. Fig. 11f exhibits the time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectrum of the MB solution at 664 nm after degradation over NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50.

It is well known that several active substances including electrons (e−), holes (h+), superoxide radicals (
O2−) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH) can be generated during the photocatalytic process, among which e− is a key substance for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). In order to investigate the possible reaction mechanism on the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites, trapping experiments of active species during photoreduction was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 12 a, when EDTA-2Na and BQ were present, the photoreduction ability for Cr(VI) of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 was greatly improved. One is because EDTA-2Na can directly consume h+, and the other is because BQ may be photodegraded to indirectly consume h+. In general, reducing h+ is beneficial to promote the separation of photoinduced e−/h+ pairs and finally increase the photocatalytic reduction rate of Cr(VI) [68]. In contrast, there was no significant change in reduction efficiency after IPA addition, suggesting that
OH is not a vital active substance for the photoreduction of Cr(VI). Surprisingly, the addition of K2S2O8 only slightly reduced the reduction efficiency, that's because the CDs in the composites have both the electron-accepting and -donating performances, and its fast electron transfer capability is also important [69,70].

In the light of the aforementioned research and analysis, the tentative mechanism of photo-reduction of Cr(VI) and photodegradation of MB over the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composite was inferred (Fig. 13 ). Under short-wavelength visible light irradiation, there are two excitation pathways in NH2-MIL-88B(Fe): (1) Fe-O clusters can be directly excited to generate photogenerated electrons and holes; (2) NH2 functional groups on the organic linker can also be excited and transfer photoelectrons to the Fe-O cluster (ie, the LMCT mechanism), and these two mechanisms work synergistically [71]. From Mott-Schottky plots, it is concluded that the CDs loaded on the surface of the MOF do not substantially change the band structure of the composite. In addition, the CB value of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs is −0.26 eV, which is more negative than the Cr(VI)/Cr(OH)3 potential (−0.13 eV vs NHE, pH = 6.8), implying that it can easily reduce Cr(VI) from a thermodynamic point of view [72,73]. Accordingly, we speculate that electrons from the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs valence band are photoexcited onto their conduction bands and leave holes in the valence band, and then photogenerated electrons reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III), the holes are dedicated to photodegradation of MB. Meanwhile, CDs generated in situ on the MOF surface by solvothermal can act as local electron acceptors to effectively enhance charge transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination, thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency of MOF. More importantly, CDs are also very good electron donors that provide more electrons for the reduction of Cr(VI) and can consume excess holes.

The reusability and stability of photocatalysts are critical for their industrial applications and are explored by NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 photoreduction of Cr(VI) reproducibility experiments. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was recovered by centrifugation and used for the next cycle. Fig. 12b reveals that after four cycles, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) is still maintained at about 77%. Moreover, the chemical stability of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CD-50 was investigated by XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy after repeated use for 4 time, and the results are displayed in Fig. 12c and d. Although the “breathing effect” of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) slightly reduced the intensity of the XRD diffraction peak, but its main diffraction peak still existed, indicating that its structure was not destroyed [74,75]. The FT-IR spectra of the sample before and after reaction was roughly consistent, indicating that the functional groups in the material were not interfered by the photocatalytic process. Finally, the FE-SEM results of Fig. 12 e and f show that CDs remain attached to the MOF surface after the photocatalytic reaction. In summary, NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites have excellent chemical stability and reusability, and have broad industrial application prospects.

In summary, a one-pot strategy for the preparation of NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composite was successfully developed by a simple solvothermal synthesis method. The synthesized NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs composites exhibit stronger photocatalytic activity than the parent NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) in both photoreduction of Cr(VI) and photodegradation of MB. This is mainly because CDs in the composite as local electron acceptors can effectively enhance charge transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination. It is worth noting that excessive loading of CDs affects the crystallization and light absorption properties of MOF and inhibits the photocatalytic activity of composites. Importantly, cycling experiments have also revealed that NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs photocatalysts have good chemical stability and reusability, and have broad industrial application prospects.
